title: Puppy Training Timeline (2026) – Month-by-Month Development Guide
excerpt: Complete puppy training timeline from 8 weeks to 12 months. What to teach, when to teach it, and realistic expectations for each stage.
quickAnswer: Start training at 8 weeks with potty training and name recognition. Introduce basic commands at 10 weeks, begin socialization before 14 weeks, and proof commands with distractions by 6 months. Full obedience reliability takes 12–18 months of consistent practice.
whoThisIsFor: New puppy parents wanting a structured training plan
Owners unsure what to teach at each age

sections.heading: Month 1 (8–12 Weeks): Foundation Phase
sections.content:
This is the most important training month. Everything you establish now creates the framework for all future learning.

**Priority skills**:
- **Potty training**: Every 2 hours + after meals, naps, and play. Mark and reward every outdoor success. See our [potty training guide](/guides/dog-potty-training-complete-guide) for detailed protocols.
- **Crate training**: Feed all meals in the crate. Build up to 1-hour naps with the door closed.
- **Name recognition**: Say name → treat when puppy looks at you. 10 reps, 3x daily.
- **Handling**: Touch paws, ears, mouth, and tail daily with treats.

**Commands to introduce**:
- Sit (lure method)
- Come (short distances, indoors only)
- Leave it (start with low-value items on the floor)

**Socialization priorities**:
- New people (aim for 5 per week)
- New surfaces (grass, tile, wood, concrete)
- Car rides (short trips with positive associations)
- Sound exposure (recordings at low volume during meals)

**Realistic expectations**: Your puppy will have accidents daily. They'll forget "sit" between sessions. They'll bite your hands. All of this is normal at 8–12 weeks. Consistency matters more than perfection.

Stock up on [puppy training essentials](/collections/puppy-essentials) before this phase begins.

sections.heading: Month 2–3 (12–20 Weeks): Skill Building
sections.content:
Your puppy's attention span is growing. Training sessions can extend to 5–7 minutes, and you can introduce more complex behaviors.

**New commands**:
- Down (lure from sit position to floor)
- Stay (start at 3 seconds, build to 30)
- Drop it (trade game with treats)
- Wait (at doors, before meals)
- Place (go to a specific bed or mat)

**Leash training begins**:
- Introduce [harness and leash](/collections/dog-leash-control) indoors first
- Practice walking in your yard with zero distractions
- Reward loose leash with treats every 3–5 steps
- Stop moving when the leash goes tight (the "be a tree" method)

**Socialization intensifies**:
- The window closes around 14–16 weeks — maximize positive exposures
- Puppy socialization classes (with vaccination requirements)
- New environments weekly
- See our [socialization guide](/guides/puppy-socialization-guide) for the complete checklist

**Potty training progress**:
- Accidents should decrease to 1–2 per week
- Your puppy may start signaling to go out (going to the door, circling)
- Extend time between outings as bladder capacity grows

**Teething starts** (around 14–16 weeks):
- Expect increased chewing and mouthing
- Provide frozen toys, bully sticks, and appropriate chews
- Read our [chewing solutions guide](/guides/puppy-chewing-solutions)

sections.heading: Month 4–6 (16–24 Weeks): Adolescent Testing
sections.content:
Welcome to puppy adolescence — the phase that makes you question all your life choices. Your previously obedient puppy will seem to "forget" everything they learned. This is normal.

**What's happening developmentally**:
- Hormonal changes (even in spayed/neutered puppies, brain chemistry shifts)
- Independence drive increases
- Fear period #2 may occur (sudden fear of previously accepted things)
- Adult teeth coming in (teething peaks around 5 months)

**Training focus**:
- **Proofing** existing commands with mild distractions
- **Recall** training in fenced areas (critical safety skill)
- **Impulse control** games (wait for food, wait at doors, leave treats on the floor)
- **Loose-leash walking** in public environments

**Common challenges**:
- Selective hearing (your puppy chooses when to respond)
- Increased pulling on leash
- Counter surfing and garbage raiding
- Jumping on visitors

**The fix**: Go back to basics. Increase reinforcement rate (more treats, more praise). Shorten sessions. Train in lower-distraction environments and gradually increase difficulty. Don't mistake adolescent rebellion for a training failure — your foundation is still there.

For professional-grade [training tools](/collections/dog-training-accessories) to support this challenging phase.

sections.heading: Month 7–12: Reliability Building
sections.content:
The adolescent storm begins to calm. Your puppy's impulse control improves, their attention span lengthens, and previously inconsistent behaviors start becoming reliable.

**Advanced skills to introduce**:
- Heel (formal position walking)
- Extended stays (2+ minutes, with you out of sight)
- Recall with distractions (other dogs, squirrels, food on ground)
- Settle command (lie down and relax in public settings)

**Real-world proofing**:
- Practice all commands in 5+ different locations
- Train around other dogs at a distance
- Practice in pet-friendly stores and outdoor cafés
- Work on car manners and vet visit behavior

**Potty training milestones**:
- By 8–10 months, most puppies are reliably house trained
- Accidents may still occur during excitement or if routine changes
- If accidents persist past 10 months, consult your veterinarian

**Exercise needs increase**:
- Most breeds can handle structured 30–45 minute walks by 8 months
- Mental exercise remains equally important (puzzle toys, training sessions, sniff walks)
- Avoid high-impact exercise (jumping, stair running) until growth plates close (12–18 months for most breeds)

**Graduation criteria**: Your puppy reliably responds to 6 core commands (sit, down, stay, come, leave it, drop it) in 3+ different environments with moderate distractions. If you've hit this milestone, congratulations — you've built a well-trained dog.

For ongoing training support, explore our [training tools collection](/collections/dog-training-accessories) and [leash control options](/collections/dog-leash-control).

faq.question: What should I teach my puppy first?
faq.answer: Start with potty training, crate training, and name recognition. These are functional life skills your puppy needs immediately. Formal commands like sit and come can begin within the first week but aren't as urgent.

faq.question: Why does my 5-month-old puppy suddenly ignore commands?
faq.answer: This is normal adolescent behavior. Your puppy's brain is undergoing developmental changes similar to a human teenager. Go back to basics, increase treat value, and train in low-distraction environments. The foundation is still there.

faq.question: How long should puppy training sessions be?
faq.answer: For puppies under 16 weeks, keep sessions to 3–5 minutes. From 4–6 months, extend to 5–7 minutes. By 8 months, most puppies can handle 10–15 minute sessions. Always end while your puppy is still engaged.

faq.question: When should I start leash training my puppy?
faq.answer: Introduce the harness and leash indoors at 10–12 weeks. Begin outdoor leash walking in your yard at 12–14 weeks. Public walks can start after second vaccination round, typically around 14–16 weeks.
